---
title: "Automation of Routine Data Scraping using CI/CD"
subtitle: "GitHub Actions & GitLab CI"
execute:
  eval: false
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: white
    slide-number: true
    code-line-numbers: true
    highlight-style: github
    embed-resources: true
author: ""
date: today
---

## Learning Objectives
::: {.incremental}
- Discover what CI/CD is and specifically how it can be used for automation
- Go through examples of using CI for routine data scraping
- Tips, tricks, and lessons learned
:::


## Disclaimer

- Code repository was forked from web scraping presentation by Matthew Hunt back in April 👏
- Additional work on the forked repository was done with assistance from LLM 🤖
- Outline of this presentation was also created by LLM 🤖


## Motivations
### Why automate data collection?

- Public health surveillance requires **regular, timely** data updates
- Manual downloads are error-prone and hard to audit
- Reproducibility demands documented, version-controlled workflows

- It's just painful to have to run the same thing over and over again


## What is CI/CD?^[https://github.com/resources/articles/ci-cd]

- Stands for "Continuous Integration" and "Continuous Deployment"
- Tools & practices for enhancing the speed and reliability of software development cycle (build, test, deploy)
- In practice, it can be used to automate anything as long as it is feasible


## CI/CD Pipeline
### What it is intended for
```{mermaid}
%%| eval: true
flowchart LR
  Plan --> Code
  Code --> Build
  Build --> Test
  Test --> Release
  Release --> Deploy
  Deploy --> Monitor
  Monitor --> Plan
```

### What I use it for
```{mermaid}
%%| eval: true
flowchart LR
  Code --> Action[Automated job]
  Action --> Pull[Get job results]
  Pull --> Integrate[Use job results]
```


## How it works
![](https://suresoft.gitlab-pages.rz.tu-bs.de/workshop-website/_images/ci-gitlab.JPG)
```{mermaid}
%%| eval: true
flowchart LR
  Code --> Remote[GitLab/GitHub]
  Remote --|trigger|--> Job
  Job --> Runner
  Runner <--> Artifacts
  Artifacts --> Ingest
```


## Why CI/CD?

- Scheduled execution without human intervention
- Consistent environments (no "works on my machine")
- Artifact storage provides data lineage


## Pre-requisites

::: {.incremental}
- Check if you have a GitHub/GitLab runner instance available
- Generate read_api personal access token (PAT) from GitHub/GitLab
- Job artifacts are enabled
- Check Feasibility
- Security compliance
:::

## Feasibility

GitLab.com CI/CD limits (may differ for each platform/instance)

- 100MB per job artifact
- 400 compute minutes per month per project
- \# of jobs, pipeline triggers, etc.

Runner must be able to access everything it needs for the job 


## Security compliance

- Make sure you are not transferring protected data to runner instances hosted outside the Agency!
- Even if you are using CI on and on-prem instance of GitLab, refrain from using it to handle protected data, especially when using docker containers. 


## Example 1: HTML Scraping

Example by Matthew Hunt back in April to scrape tables off [Canada Respiratory Virus Surveillance](https://health-infobase.canada.ca/respiratory-virus-surveillance/influenza.html)

- **Dynamic content** — Tables load via JavaScript; static `requests.get()` won't work
- **Headless browsers in CI** — Must configure `--headless`, `--no-sandbox`, `--disable-dev-shm-usage`
- **Fragile selectors** — Website redesigns break CSS selectors
- **Pagination** — Need to click through pages to collect all data
- **Rate limiting** — Respectful delays between requests


## Example 2: GraphQL queries to PHAC Wastewater Infobase^[https://github.com/PHACDataHub/wastewater-graphql]

- **Endpoint:** `https://api-ipa.hc-sc.gc.ca/wastewater/`
- **No API key required** — Public data
- **Predictable schema** — No parsing HTML

```graphql
{
  Infobase {
    Date
    Location
    region
    viral_load
    seven_day_rolling_avg
    pruid
  }
}
```


## Example 2: Implementation

### R (`httr2` + `jsonlite`)

```{r}
#| echo: true

resp <- request(endpoint) |>
  req_body_json(list(query = query)) |>
  req_perform()
records <- fromJSON(resp_body_string(resp))$data$Infobase
write.csv(records, "data/wastewater_infobase_r.csv")
```


## Configuring GitLab CI pipelines or GitHub Actions workflows

- Consult the docs
- or ask AI to help you 🤖


## GitHub Actions

```yaml
name: Web Scraping CI Pipeline
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1'  # Weekly

jobs:
  python-scrape:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
      - uses: browser-actions/setup-chrome@v1
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: python src/Web_scraping_with_Python.py
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
```

## GitLab CI

```yaml
r-graphql:
  stage: graphql
  image: rocker/tidyverse:4.4
  before_script:
    - apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev
  script:
    - Rscript src/query_wastewater_R.R
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - data/
    expire_in: 30 days
```

Docker images provide isolated, reproducible environments.


## Pipeline artifacts

- Downloadable from GitHub Actions / GitLab CI
- Retention policy
- Versioned by run timestamp

## How to retrieve the results

There is a package called `gitlabr` which has a number of issues.
Alternatively, just use API requests - it's actually pretty simple!

1. Get a personal access token generated with read_api
2. Store your token in an environment file NOT in your script
3. Use API to figure out the project id and job id
4. Use API to retrieve the job artifact


## Function for staging artifacts
```{r}
#| echo: true
artifacts_to_staging <- function(
    job_id,
    token=gl_token,
    gitlab_url="https://gitlab.cscscience.ca", 
    project_id=1662L
    ){
  require(utils)
  require(httr2)
  
  filename <- tempfile(fileext=".zip")
  on.exit(file.remove(filename))
  
  request(gitlab_url) |>
    req_url_path_append("api", "v4", "projects", project_id, "jobs", job_id, "artifacts") |>
    req_headers(
      "PRIVATE-TOKEN" = token
    ) |>
    req_perform() |> 
    resp_body_raw() |> 
    writeBin(filename)
  
  utils::unzip(filename, junkpaths=TRUE, exdir="staging", overwrite = TRUE)
}

# unzip artifacts to staging folder
artifacts_to_staging(52406L)
```


## Next steps 

- Automate more data collection!
- Ingest into a database/datalake


## Lessons learned

- Using docker (rocker) will make your life easier and the pipeline more reproducible
  - depending on the runner setup, you don't have a choice and must use docker
- Don't expect a successful run the first time, especially with LLM
- If using GitHub actions, check action versions
- Check your artifacts!

## Acknowledgements

- Matthew Hunt
- NMLB SIS team
- PHAC R User Group
